# Agent Anatomy

> Interactive visual essays explaining how AI systems actually work, for readers with no AI background. Episode 01, "How an AI agent works," teaches the agent loop (think → act → observe → repeat), the context window as a finite token budget, and tool use — through a scroll-driven WebGL essay and an interactive replay of a scripted agent run fixing a broken product page. Episode 1.5, "Where agents go wrong," is the darker companion: three failure modes — a loop trap, a context overflow that evicts the original request, and a bad observation the agent catches by verifying. Episode 02, "How AI reads the web," shows retrieval: an agent that doesn't know the answer searches the web, weighs the results, reads the trustworthy pages (and hits one it can't read), and writes an answer with citations threaded back to its sources. Episode 03, "How agents remember," is the season finale: it separates context from memory — an agent plans a Tokyo trip across two sessions, compacts a full context window into a smaller, lossy summary, writes durable notes to a file, ends the session, and then in a fresh empty window the next day reads those notes back and finishes the job.

The project ships two things: the essay, and an open trace format + replay engine. A trace is a JSON file describing one complete agent run (system prompt, user message, thinking, tool calls, tool results with artifact snapshots, assistant message — each with a token estimate). The headless replay engine renders any trace that fits the schema. The 1.1 schema adds a context-eviction event and optional authorial annotations; the 1.2 schema adds web search, page fetches (which may come back "unreadable"), a sources registry, and citations binding answer spans to sources; the 1.3 schema adds compaction (compressing context into a lossy summary), session breaks (emptying the window), and memory read/write events (notes that live outside the window as files). Four episodes, one engine, from one backward-compatible format — proof it generalizes.

Made by Wenhao Yu (wenhaoyu.bryan@gmail.com). MIT licensed.

## Pages

- [Series index](https://wenhaoyu-bryan.github.io/agent-anatomy/): all episodes and their status
- [Episode 01 — How an AI agent works](https://wenhaoyu-bryan.github.io/agent-anatomy/episodes/how-an-agent-works/): the full interactive essay
- [Episode 1.5 — Where agents go wrong](https://wenhaoyu-bryan.github.io/agent-anatomy/episodes/where-agents-go-wrong/): three failure modes (loop trap, context overflow, bad-observation recovery) as short replays
- [Episode 02 — How AI reads the web](https://wenhaoyu-bryan.github.io/agent-anatomy/episodes/how-ai-reads-the-web/): web retrieval — search, source selection, reading a page, and citations, with an interactive replay of a "is it safe to reheat rice?" run
- [Episode 03 — How agents remember](https://wenhaoyu-bryan.github.io/agent-anatomy/episodes/how-agents-remember/): context vs. memory — compaction, session breaks, and notes written to files, with a two-session replay of a Tokyo-trip plan that survives an empty window

## Repository

- [Source on GitHub](https://github.com/wenhaoyu-bryan/agent-anatomy): schema, engine, traces, and the essay
- [Trace format spec](https://github.com/wenhaoyu-bryan/agent-anatomy/blob/main/docs/trace-format.md): how to write your own trace file
- [JSON Schema](https://github.com/wenhaoyu-bryan/agent-anatomy/blob/main/docs/trace.schema.json): machine-readable trace schema
